skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents via inference.sh. Navigate web pages, interact with elements using @e refs, take screenshots, record video. Capabilities: web scraping, form filling, clicking, typing, drag-drop, file upload, JavaScript execution. Use for: web automation, data extraction, testing, agent browsing, research. Triggers: browser, web automation, scrape, navigate, click, fill form, screenshot, browse web, playwright, headless browser, web agent, surf internet, record video
skill.summary: Browser automation for AI agents via inference.sh with Playwright. Navigate web pages, interact with elements using @e refs, take screenshots, record video.
skill.author: toolshell

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate browser automation skill that uses inference.sh with Playwright. The static findings (external_commands, network, filesystem) are expected behavior for browser automation and represent documentation examples showing CLI usage, not actual security vulnerabilities. No malicious intent detected.

high_findings.title: Heuristic Warning: Browser Automation Capabilities
high_findings.description: The skill combines browser automation, network access, and credential handling. This is expected behavior for a browser automation tool and represents legitimate functionality.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a heuristic flag that triggers on browser automation tools. The combination of network, filesystem, and external commands is standard for browser automation and not indicative of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation shows example shell commands using infsh CLI. These are documentation examples, not actual code execution vulnerabilities.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backtick and shell command patterns appear in documentation showing how to use the infsh CLI tool. These are example commands for users to follow, not arbitrary command execution.

low_findings.title: Network Access for Browser Automation
low_findings.description: The skill requires network access to navigate websites. This is expected behavior for browser automation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Browser automation inherently requires network access to load web pages. This is the primary function of the skill.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access for Screenshots and Videos
low_findings.description: The skill can save screenshots and recordings to filesystem. This is expected functionality for a browser automation tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Saving screenshots and video recordings is a core feature of browser automation tools. The files are saved to user-specified locations.

content.value_statement: Enable AI agents to automate web browsing tasks including form filling, data extraction, screenshot capture, and video recording through a simple command-line interface.
actual_capabilities: Navigate to URLs and interact with web pages programmatically
Fill forms, click elements, and perform drag-drop operations using element references
Capture screenshots and record browser sessions as video
Execute JavaScript code in the browser context
Manage multiple browser sessions with proxy support
Extract data from web pages through element inspection
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI (infsh) to be installed and configured
Relies on external inference.sh service for browser control
Cannot bypass CAPTCHAs or advanced bot detection systems

use_cases.title: Automated Web Testing
use_cases.description: AI agents can navigate to web applications, fill test forms, verify UI elements, and capture test results as screenshots or video.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers automating browser-based tests

use_cases.title: Data Extraction and Research
use_cases.description: Extract structured data from websites by navigating pages, identifying elements, and collecting information programmatically.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and data analysts gathering web data

use_cases.title: Form Automation Workflows
use_cases.description: Automate repetitive form filling tasks like data entry, application submissions, and bulk operations across multiple pages.
use_cases.target_user: Business users automating repetitive web tasks

prompt_templates.title: Open Website and Get Elements
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the browser automation skill to open https://example.com and show me all the interactive elements on the page with their references.

prompt_templates.title: Fill Form and Submit
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the login page at [URL], fill in the email field with user@example.com, fill the password field with mypassword, then click the submit button. Take a screenshot after submission.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Data from Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the page at [URL], identify all table rows in the data table, and extract the text content from each row. Return the data as a structured list.
prompt_templates.scenario: Scrape structured data from web tables

prompt_templates.title: Record Workflow Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a new browser session with video recording enabled. Navigate through these steps: [list steps], then close the session and provide the video file path.
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a browser automation walkthrough video

output_examples.output:
Session started with ID: abc123

Interactive elements found:
- @e1 [a] "Example Domain" href="/"
- @e2 [h1] "Example Domain"
- @e3 [p] "This domain is for use in illustrative examples..."
- @e4 [a] "More information..." href="https://www.iana.org/domains/example"

output_examples.input: Take a screenshot of the current page
output_examples.output:
Screenshot saved to: /tmp/screenshot_20240115_143022.png

Page title: Example Domain
Viewport: 1280x720

best_practices: Use element references (@e1, @e2) for reliable element targeting instead of CSS selectors
Take snapshots after each navigation or significant page change to get fresh element references
Enable video recording for debugging complex automation workflows
Use proxy settings when testing geo-restricted content or need anonymity
anti_patterns: Do not rely on element positions or coordinates - use @e refs instead for stable targeting
Avoid long wait times; use explicit waits for element visibility rather than fixed delays
Do not skip re-snapshotting after page navigation - element refs become stale
Avoid uploading sensitive files without verifying the target website accepts uploads

faq.question: What is inference.sh and do I need an account?
faq.answer: Inference.sh is the underlying service that provides browser automation capabilities. You need to install the infsh CLI and configure it with your account credentials to use this skill.

faq.question: Can this skill bypass login forms or CAPTCHAs?
faq.answer: No, this skill cannot bypass authentication systems or CAPTCHAs. It can only interact with web pages programmatically after you provide credentials or when authentication is already handled.

faq.question: How do element references (@e1, @e2) work?
faq.answer: Element references are assigned by the snapshot function. Each time you call snapshot, you get a fresh list of interactive elements with their @e prefixes. Use these references in subsequent interact commands.

faq.question: Can I run multiple browser sessions simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes, each session has a unique session ID. You can manage multiple sessions in parallel by using different session identifiers.

faq.question: What browsers are supported?
faq.answer: The skill uses Playwright under the hood, supporting Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit. The default is Chromium for maximum compatibility.

faq.question: How do I handle dynamic content that loads slowly?
faq.answer: Use the 'wait' action with milliseconds, or use the 'wait_for' option in the interact function. You can also execute JavaScript to wait for specific conditions.
